title: Fixing CSS Media Query Priority with Inconsistent Feature Order

statement.markdown: Implement a CSS media query parser and fix the bug that occurs when the media features are in an inconsistent order. This function should take a CSS media query string as input and return the parsed media query object with the correct priority.
statement.inputFormat: A CSS media query string, e.g. '(max-width: 768px) and (min-height: 1024px)'
statement.outputFormat: A media query object with the following structure: `{features: [...], priority: number}`
statement.notes: Assume the media query string is well-formed and the features are separated by the 'and' keyword.

constraints.description: Number of media features in the query
Number of queries in the input string

examples.explanation: The feature "max-width" has a higher priority than "min-height"
The feature "min-height" has a higher priority than "max-width"

hints.text: Split the media query string into individual features and queries.
Compare the features based on their names and values.
Assign a priority to each query based on the comparison result.

editorial.title: Fixing CSS Media Query Priority with Inconsistent Feature Order
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to compare the features based on their names and values.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible approach is to use a dictionary to store the features and their corresponding priorities.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient solution is to use a custom comparator to compare the features.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a priority queue to store the features and their corresponding priorities.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is based on the comparison result of the features.
commonMistakes: Comparing features based on their values only
Failing to handle cases with multiple queries
